The barometer is plummeting faster than I've ever seen, and the sky has turned a bruised purple.
I need to double-reef the mainsail and lash down every loose crate before the surge hits us.
This swell is building far too quickly for us to make it back to the harbor safely.
We have to batten down the hatches and pray the hull holds against these monstrous breaking waves.
The wind is screaming through the rigging, and I can barely hear the crew shouting over the gale.
The stars are shifting in the sky, and I cannot find the North Star to guide my course.
I've sailed these waters for twenty years, but I've never seen the horizon glow a sickly violet like this.
If the tides turn erratic because of that flickering light in the heavens, we'll be driven straight onto the reefs.
May the gods have mercy on us, for the constellations are folding into themselves like wet parchment.
I need to batten down the hatches and secure the rigging before the wind follows that strange celestial fire.
You can't claim these waters when my charts clearly show the deep channel belongs to my fleet.
I don't care for your shore maps when my anchor is already set in this harbor.
We've navigated these currents for generations and won't let some landlubber tell us where to moor.
If you keep pushing your boundary lines into my fishing grounds, we'll have a real storm brewing.
My crew and I have bled for this coastline and we aren't yielding a single fathom of it.
May the gods preserve us, for there is a shimmering rift in the water where no current should flow.
I've sailed every coast from the Frozen Reach to the Southern Isles, but I've never seen a light that breathes like that.
Steady the helm and drop the anchor, lest the tide pull us headlong into that swirling void of violet glass.
The compass needle is spinning like a madman, and the air tastes of ozone and salt from a sea I don't recognize.
I'll not steer my crew into a maw of light that defies every chart and map in the captain's cabin.
This green tide of brush is thicker than any fog I have ever steered through.
I cannot find a steady current in these woods to guide my bearing toward the coast.
The canopy blocks the stars, leaving me without a sextant to mark my true position.
I keep searching for a break in the treeline that looks like an open channel.
My boots are sinking into this loam like a hull dragging through a shallow sandbar.
The wind has died completely, leaving the canvas slack and the ocean like a mirror.
I have forgotten the sound of another man's voice since we left the last port.
Every hour is the same, just the creak of the hull and the slapping tide.
I find myself talking to the gulls just to keep my tongue from rotting away.
The horizon remains empty and the doldrums have swallowed every scrap of sound for weeks.
Batten down the hatches and get those guns primed before they close the distance.
You'll find no quarter here, you scurvy dog, unless you strike your colors now.
Steady as she goes, keep the wind at our backs or we're sunk.
Man the rigging and prepare to repel boarders before they hook onto our rail.
I've weathered gales worse than you, and I'll send you straight to the locker.
This brass sextant was my grandfather's, and it has guided me through a thousand leagues of open water.
I keep my weathered leather logbook tucked dry in the locker, for it holds every gale I have survived.
This heavy wool pea coat is the only thing keeping the biting salt spray from freezing my skin.
My trusty folding knife has cut more rigging and spliced more lines than I can count in my years.
This gold pocket watch still ticks true, reminding me of the harbor I left behind so many winters ago.
We have to ration the fresh water now or we will be parched before we sight land.
I must keep a tight watch on the remaining salt pork if the crew keeps sneaking extra.
The wind has died completely and we are burning through our last few casks of oil for light.
We cannot afford to waste a single inch of canvas while the storm is ripping the mainsail.
I will have to limit the rum rations today to keep the men focused on the rigging.
